coredata.dc:title: Effect of reverse pulse current density on microstructure and properties of supercritical Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings

coredata.dc:description: In this study, Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings were prepared by double-pulse electrodeposition under supercritical CO2 with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the second phase additive. The effects of supercritical CO2 conditions and reverse pulse current density on microstructure, crystal orientation, grain size, GQDs quality, mechanical properties, and corrosion resistance of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings were investigated. The results show that when the reverse pulse current density is 0.8 A /dm2, the surface of Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coating is compact and flat, GQDs is uniformly dispersed in the coating, and GQDs is closely bound to Ni grains. Compared with the coating prepared at normal temperature and pressure. The grain size of the Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coating is 4.58 nm, and the grain size is reduced by 75.3 %. The quality of GQDs in the coating was improved. The coating hardness is 867.22 HV, which is significantly increased by 53.7 %. The roughness is 0.236 μm, which is significantly reduced by 37.2 %. The friction coefficient and volume wear were 0.262 and 3.395 × 107 μm3, respectively, which were significantly reduced by 27.4 % and 57.9 %. After electrochemical corrosion, the self-corrosion voltage of the coating was −139 mV, and the self-corrosion current density was 3.19 × 10−7 A/cm2. The self-corrosion voltage was significantly increased by 61.2 %, and the self-corrosion current density was significantly decreased by 71.2 %. The R ct value and N dl value of the coating are 31594.53 Ω·cm2 and 0.862, respectively. Significantly increased by 226.2 % and 67.1 %, respectively. The coating has excellent mechanical properties and corrosion resistance.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings were prepared by double-pulse electrodeposition under supercritical CO2 with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the second phase additive. The effects of supercritical CO2 conditions and reverse pulse current density on microstructure, crystal orientation, grain size, GQDs quality, mechanical properties, and corrosion resistance of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings were investigated. The results show that when the reverse pulse current density is 0.8 A /dm2, the surface of Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coating is compact and flat, GQDs is uniformly dispersed in the coating, and GQDs is closely bound to Ni grains. Compared with the coating prepared at normal temperature and pressure. The grain size of the Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coating is 4.58 nm, and the grain size is reduced by 75.3 %. The quality of GQDs in the coating was improved. The coating hardness is 867.22 HV, which is significantly increased by 53.7 %. The roughness is 0.236 μm, which is significantly reduced by 37.2 %. The friction coefficient and volume wear were 0.262 and 3.395 × 107 μm3, respectively, which were significantly reduced by 27.4 % and 57.9 %. After electrochemical corrosion, the self-corrosion voltage of the coating was −139 mV, and the self-corrosion current density was 3.19 × 10−7 A/cm2. The self-corrosion voltage was significantly increased by 61.2 %, and the self-corrosion current density was significantly decreased by 71.2 %. The R ct value and N dl value of the coating are 31594.53 Ω·cm2 and 0.862, respectively. Significantly increased by 226.2 % and 67.1 %, respectively. The coating has excellent mechanical properties and corrosion resistance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal and dispersed particles from suspension electrolytes form a composite electrochemical coating during electrodeposition. Nickel is one of the most common single metal coatings. As a decorative or functional coating, it can improve surface gloss and strengthen surface wear and corrosion resistance. Nickel-based nanocomposite coatings are characterized by higher hardness, better stability in various corrosion environments, and better adhesion to the substrate on the basis of the original nickel coatings, so they are widely used in composite electrochemical coatings [1–5]. Haixiang Chen et al. [6] introduced soluble rare earth compounds CeCl3 or LaCl3 in order to optimize the microstructure and properties of nickel electroplating and electrodeposited on copper substrate by direct current (DC) and pulse current (PC) methods, respectively. The results show that the PC-electrodeposited nickel /LaCl3 films exhibit the highest hardness (320 HV), the lowest tensile stress (55 MPa), and the best electrochemical passivation protection, which effectively improves the comprehensive properties of nickel films and has a good industrial application prospect.

Supercritical fluid technology [7–10] applies fluids higher than critical pressure and temperature. Supercritical fluid has no obvious gas–liquid interface and is in a state of gas–liquid separation. It has unique physical characteristics, low viscosity, high density, good mass transfer, heat transfer, flow, and dissolution performance. It is widely used in energy saving, extraction of natural products, polymerization reactions, and catalytic processes. Supercritical CO2 condition means that the CO2 pressure is higher than 7.39 MPa and higher than 31.26℃, the critical condition of CO2 is easy to reach, and the chemical properties of CO2 are not active, non-toxic and harmless, low price and easy to obtain. Supercritical CO2 technology [11–14] is widely used in functional component extraction, semiconductor cleaning, dry cleaning technology, and chemical reactions. Under the condition of supercritical CO2, the addition of surfactants makes the water-based electrolyte form a supercritical emulsion with excellent conduction properties. After electrodeposition, the surface of the coating is brighter and smoother, and the grains are finer. Supercritical CO2 significantly improves the coating's surface quality and mechanical properties [15–17].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [18–20] are a new carbon fluorescent material. The graphene layer size is less than 100 nm, and the number of layers is less than ten layers. It has the advantages of low cost, simple preparation, low toxicity, and good light stability. GQDs also have the characteristics of high electrical conductivity, excellent mechanical strength, and thermal stability and are applied in biomedicine, optoelectronics, electronic devices, and other fields [21–24]. When the GQDs are used as the second phase additive to prepare the nickel base composite coating under supercritical CO2, the addition of GQDs can improve the surface quality of the coating and the mechanical properties and corrosion resistance [25].

Compared with direct current electrodeposition, by adjusting the pulse duty cycle, frequency, and other parameters, a higher peak current can be used to refine the grain size, and it is easier to obtain nanocomposite coatings and improve the surface quality and mechanical properties of the coatings. Pulse electrodeposition technology has the advantages of high efficiency, economy, and environmental protection, and the coating has excellent physical and chemical properties, which are widely used in optoelectronics, electronics, the chemical industry, and medical fields [26–28]. Compared with single-pulse electrodeposition, double-pulse electrodeposition [29,30] introduces a set of reverse pulse currents, which can remove the defects such as bumps, burrs, and nodules on the surface of the coating during electrodeposition. Double pulse electrodeposition makes the thickness of the coating more uniform, the surface more compact and flat, and also plays a role in improving the mechanical properties of the coating. Dongai Wang et al. [31] studied the effects of double pulse positive and negative duty cycle and reverse working time on the properties of Ni/nanodiamond composite coatings. The experimental results show that when the nanodiamond particle content in the plating solution is 5 g/L, the reverse pulse working time, forward pulse duty cycle, and reverse pulse duty cycle of the double pulse electroplating parameters are 20 ms, 0.3, and 0.2, respectively. The composite coatings prepared by double pulse have good comprehensive properties.

In this paper, Ni-GODs nanocomposite coatings were prepared by double-pulse electrodeposition process under supercritical CO2 conditions using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with unique properties as the second phase additive. In the experimental study, the optimal experimental parameter was preliminarily determined: the additional amount of GQDs was 1.5 g/L. The forward pulse current density is 5 A/dm2. The forward pulse duty cycle is 0.25. The reverse pulse duty cycle is 0.25. The frequency is 1000 Hz. The pressure is 10.5 MPa. The temperature is 50℃; The plating time is 60 min. In order to study the effects of reverse pulse current density and supercritical CO2 conditions on the microstructure and properties of Ni-GODs nanocomposite coatings, four kinds of Ni-GODs nanocomposite coatings were prepared by different processes. Subsequently, the surface morphology, crystal orientation, grain size, mechanical properties, and corrosion resistance of the coating were compared and analyzed, and the most suitable reverse pulse current density was explored, which provided a basis for the optimization of double-pulse electrodeposition process under supercritical CO2 conditions and the study of new nickel-based nanocomposites.

The supercritical CO2-assisted electrodeposition device is shown in Fig. 1 . The device was independently designed by our research group. By controlling the temperature of supercritical fluid, the pressure of the reactor, and the temperature of the electroplating solution through a cooler, pressure pump, and temperature control system, the electrodeposition under the condition of supercritical CO2 is realized. The power supply adopts an intelligent multi-group commutation pulse plating power supply (Handan Dashun Plating Equipment Co., Ltd.).

Experimental method
(1)
The cathode and anode of the electrodeposited fixture are treated to meet the requirements of the experiment.
(2)
Configure graphene quantum dots (GQDs), the second phase additive required for electrodeposition.
(3)
The plating solution used for electrodeposition is configured.
(4)
Electrodeposition is carried out after constructing supercritical CO2 conditions in the reactor.

Experimental steps:
(1)
The cathode of the fixture is a nickel plate, the anode is a copper plate, the purity of the copper plate and the nickel plate are 99.9 %, the size is 20 mm × 20 mm, and the cathode and anode are 20 mm apart. Sandpaper of 400 #, 1200 #, 2000 #, 3000 #, 5000 #, and 7000 # is used to polish the copper sheet to achieve rust removal, oil removal, and oxidation oxidation film removal. At the same time, the sandpaper of 400 # and 1200 # is used to polish the nickel sheet to smooth the surface and remove stains. Then, the copper and nickel plates meeting the experimental requirements were fixed on the electrodeposited cathode and anode, respectively, with cyanoacrylate glue.
(2)
The solution formula required for the preparation of graphene quantum dots is shown in Table 1 . According to the solution formula, use EL204 electronic balance (Mettler Toledo Instrument (Shanghai) Co., LTD.) to weigh quantitative citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O) and urea (CH4N2O) into a beaker with a capacity of 200 ml, add 100 ml ultra-pure water and stir well. Pour the mixture into the teflon reactor and place the reactor in an oven at 180 °C for 8 h. After the set time is over, the reactor solution is poured into the beaker, and then the anhydrous ethanol is poured into the mixed solution and into the centrifugal tube. The centrifuge tube was put into a centrifuge for high-speed centrifuge treatment, the waste liquid was poured out, GQDs were extracted, and GQDs were prepared by drying treatment.

(3) The formula for the electroplating solution is shown in Table 2 . According to the formula of the plating solution, the quantitative nickel sulfate (NiSO4·6H2O), nickel chloride (NiCl2·6H2O), boric acid (H3BO3), and sodium dodecyl sulfate (C12H25SO4Na) were weighed with EL204 electronic balance (Mettler Toledo Instrument (Shanghai) Co., LTD.) and placed in a 400 ml beaker. Then 100 ml of ultra-pure water was added to the beaker using a measuring cylinder, 0.1 ml of surfactant (polyethylene glycol trimethyl nonyl ether), and the magnetic rotor was placed into the beaker. According to the formula of the plating solution, the quantitative graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were weighed with an electronic balance and placed in a 100 ml capacity beaker, and 50 ml ultra-pure water was measured with a measuring cylinder and put into the beaker. Place a 400 ml beaker into the EMS-12 split-type magnetic stirrer (Tianjin Ounuo Instrument Co., Ltd.), turn on the magnetic stirring function of the EMS-12 split-type magnetic stirrer, and stir the solution. Then, place a beaker with a capacity of 100 ml into the Chunlin Ultrasonic Cleaning Machine (Shenzhen Chunlin Cleaning Equipment Co., Ltd.) and use the ultrasonic function to vibrate the GQDs solution. After 10 min, the GQDs solution will be completely dispersed. Pour the dispersed GQDs solution into a 400 ml beaker, turn on the ultrasonic vibration function of the EMS-12 split magnetic stirrers, and stir the solution with ultrasonic magnetic force simultaneously. After about 45 ∼ 60 min, the electroplating solution will be evenly dispersed. Make the solution meet the experimental requirements.

(4) Pour the uniformly dispersed electroplating solution into the reactor of the supercritical unit, install a fixed electrodeposition device, and then turn on the supercritical CO2 electrodeposition device, cool the CO2 gas below 5℃, control the temperature of the reactor at 50℃, and input the CO2 gas into the reactor after the temperature of the reactor is stable, and then start the pressure pump to increase the pressure. The pressure inside the reactor was controlled at 10.5 MPa so that the environment inside the reactor reached the state of supercritical CO2. Then, the Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coating was prepared by turning on the double pulse power supply.

The effect of reverse pulse current density on the organization and properties of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings was investigated using a double-pulse current electrodeposition process under supercritical CO2 conditions. Three groups of nanocomposite coatings with reverse pulse current densities of 0.3 A/dm2, 0.8 A/dm2, and 1.3 A/dm2 (Ni-GQDs-I ∼ Ni-GQDs-III) were prepared for comparative analysis. Meanwhile, in order to investigate the effect of supercritical CO2 on the organization and properties of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings. Ni-GQDs-IV nanocomposite coatings with a reverse pulse current density of 0.8 A/dm2 were prepared at normal temperature and atmospheric pressure. The Ni-GQDs-IV nanocomposite coatings were compared and analyzed with the Ni-GQDs-II nanocomposite coatings. Four different sets of electrodeposition process parameters are listed in Table 3 .

SIGMA500 field emission scanning electron microscope (Carl Zeiss AG, Germany) was used to observe and analyze the coating's surface morphology. EDS plane scanning and spot scanning were performed on the coating, and the EDS scanning results were analyzed. The coating was thinned to about 100 nm using a Gatan691 ion thinning instrument (Gatan, USA). Then, the combination of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and nickel grains in the coating was observed by the FEI Talos F200X transmission microscope (EFI Technology, USA). HD-XpertPRO X-ray diffractometer (Panalytical Company of the Netherlands) was used to test the coating. XRD used Cu target Kα radiation, the scanning Angle was 5 ∼ 80°, and the XRD pattern of the coating was analyzed and calculated after the test. The HORIBA HR Evolution Raman spectrometer (Jobin Yvon, France) determined the coating's chemical structure and analyzed the Raman spectra. TR200 surface roughness meter (Beijing Times Company) was used to measure the coating's surface roughness, and the results were analyzed. HXD-1000TMS digital microhardness tester (Beijing Shangguang Instrument Co., LTD.) was used to test the hardness of the coating. The test force was 200 gf. Five different points on the surface of the coating were tested, and the average hardness was taken for analysis.

The friction and wear of the coating were measured by Nanovea tribometer (USA Nanovea Company). A high-chromium steel bearing ball with a diameter of 6 mm was selected as the grinding part. The test parameters were as follows: The load is 10 N, the speed is 200 r/min, and the test time is 10 min. After the experiment, the friction coefficient diagram is derived and analyzed. A Nanovea PS50 optical profiler (USA Nanovea Company) was used to profile the wear marks of the coating. The scanning area is 2 mm × 2 mm, the scanning rate is 3.33 mm/s, and the step size is 5 μm. The results were analyzed after the scanning. The surface morphology of the coating wear marks was observed and analyzed by scanning electron microscopy. The electrochemistry corrosion experiment of the coating was carried out in a three-electrode system using a Metrohm PGSTAT302N electrochemical workstation (Metrohm, Switzerland). The experiment was carried out in 3.5 % NaCl solution with the platinum sheet as the counter electrode, the coating as the working electrode, and Ag/AgCl−(1 M) as the reference electrode. The scanning rate of the measured potential polarization curve is 0.5 mV/s, and the voltage range is −0.2 V ∼ 0.2 V. When the AC impedance spectrum is measured, the frequency range is 0.01 ∼ 10000 Hz, and the sine amplitude is 10 mV. The results are analyzed after the test. The coating was immersed in 3.5 % NaCl solution for 120 h, and the corrosion morphology of the coating was observed and analyzed by SEM after corrosion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reverse pulse current density on the microstructure of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows 1000-fold SEM images of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings prepared by different processes. Fig. 2 (a) ∼ 2 (c) are SEM images of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings prepared with different reverse pulse current densities under supercritical CO2 conditions. It can be seen from Fig. 2 (a) that when the reverse pulse current density is 0.3 A/dm2, the surface topography of Ni-GQDs-Ⅰ nanocomposite coating is poor, and the surface of the coating is not flat and dense. There are defects such as pores, irregular nodules, and protrusions. It can be seen from Fig. 2 (b) that when the reverse pulse current density is increased to 0.8 A/dm2, the surface of Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coating is relatively flat and dense, and there are no defects such as bumps and nodules, and the surface topography is good. It can be seen from Fig. 2 (c) that when the reverse current density is increased to 1.3 A/dm2, the surface morphology of Ni-GQDs-Ⅲ nanocomposite coating is poor, with defects such as massive spalling, surface pitting, holes, and irregular protruding.

The reasons for the above phenomenon are as follows: when the reverse pulse current density is 0.3 A/dm2, the current density is small, which leads to a low dissolution effect of the reverse pulse current on the cathode. The reverse pulse current cannot effectively remove the bumps and nodules on the Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coating. It leads to lower ion concentration at the cathode and a poorer electrodeposition effect. As a result, the surface density and flatness of Ni-GQDs-Ⅰ nanocomposite coating are poor, and there are irregular nodules and protrusions. With the increase of the reverse pulse current density to 0.8 A/dm2, the cathode dissolution effect of the reverse pulse current is increased, and the ion concentration on the cathode surface is increased, which is conducive to the role of higher forward pulse current density in the subsequent cathode cycle. At higher forward pulse current densities, the rate of crystal nucleation is greater than the rate of crystal growth, resulting in smaller grain sizes in the coating. Therefore, the surface of Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coating is relatively dense, flat, and has fewer defects. When the reverse pulse current density continues to increase to 1.3 A/dm2, the cathode dissolution effect of the reverse pulse current is too high, the surface of Ni-GQDs-Ⅲ nanocomposite coating is over-dissolved, the cathode ion concentration is too high, the effect of electrodeposition is poor, the surface of the coating has massive spalling, holes and protrusions, and the surface density of the coating is low and the flatness is poor.

Fig. 2 (d) shows the SEM image of the surface of Ni-GQDs-IV nanocomposite coating prepared at normal temperature and atmospheric pressure with a reverse pulse current density of 0.8 A/dm2. As shown in Fig. 2 (d), the surface density of the coating is poor, the flatness is low, and there are obvious defects such as bumps, holes, cracks, and so on. Due to the low conductivity and solubility of metal salts in supercritical CO2, crystal growth is hindered when supercritical CO2 comes into contact with the cathode. When the plating solution is in contact with the cathode, new atomic nuclei are formed, and crystals grow. In addition, supercritical CO2 can effectively disperse graphene quantum dots (GQDs). GQDs form an active nucleation site in the coating, and the increase of the nucleation site inhibits crystal growth and makes the grains smaller. There is no supercritical CO2-assisted electrodeposition at normal temperature and atmospheric pressure, so the surface quality of Ni-GQDs-Ⅳ nanocomposite coating is poor, and there are many defects.

In order to determine the distribution and percentage of elements in the Ni-GQDs-II nanocomposite coatings, EDS planar and spot scans of the coatings were performed using SEM. The results were also analyzed. Fig. 3 , Table 4 and Table 5 show the EDS scan results of the coatings. The surface morphology of the coating is shown in Fig. 3 (a). The EDS planar scanning results are shown in Fig. 3 (b), Fig. 3 (c), Fig. 3 (d) and Table 4. From Fig. 3 (d) and Table 4, it can be seen that the coating contains only Ni and C elements. The percentage of C element in the coating is 18.67 %. This proves the presence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in the coating. Fig. 3 (b) and Fig. 3 (c) show that Ni grains and GQDs are uniformly distributed and closely connected in the coating. However, because of the low percentage of GQDs in the coating, the distribution of Ni elements is denser and the distribution of C elements is sparse. Fig. 3(e), Fig. 3(f), Fig. 3(g) and Table 5 show the EDS scanning results of the three points in Fig. 3(a). The results show that the coating contains only Ni and C elements. The percentage of C elements in the three points is 21.21 %, 19.40 %, and 16.38 % respectively, with an average percentage of 18.99 %. This also proves the existence of GQDs.

To observe the presence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coating, TEM was used to observe the microstructure of Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coating, and TEM results are shown in Fig. 4 . It can be seen from Fig. 4 (a) that there are large differences in the size of individual grains of nickel grains. Still, the overall size of the grains is relatively uniform; all of them are in the range of nanometer grain size, and the connection between the Ni grains is relatively tight, and there are obvious grain boundaries. It can be seen from Fig. 4 (b) that GQDs particles can be found in the coating under TEM at higher multiples, indicating that GQDs are successfully deposited on the coating, and GQDs are closely associated with Ni grains, indicating that the properties of GQDs do not change and no interface reaction occurs. This good interface combination is conducive to the transfer of loading force between the coating and the substrate and then improves the mechanical properties of the coating. Fig. 4 (c) shows the SADP diagram at point A. The white points in the diagram are arranged regularly and distributed symmetrically relative to the center point, indicating that the large-sized Ni grains of the composite coating are in a single crystal state and there is no subgrain boundary, which is consistent with the results of the XRD pattern. Fig. 4 (d) shows the EDS diagram at point B in the middle of Fig. 4. It can be seen from Fig. 4 that the proportion of C element and Ni element at point B is 25.23 % and 77.77 %, respectively, which is consistent with the EDS point scanning results of SEM, proving that the C element exists in the coating, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are successfully embedded in the composite coating and combined between Ni grains.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of reverse pulse current density on the preferred orientation and grain size of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 shows the XRD patterns of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings prepared by different processes. It can be seen from Fig. 5 that the diffraction peak Angle of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings prepared under supercritical CO2 conditions is the same. The crystal faces corresponding to each diffraction peak are (111), (200), and (220), respectively, indicating that the crystal structure of Ni is a face-centered cubic structure [32]. The diffraction intensities of (111) and (200) faces of Ni-GQDs-Ⅳ nanocomposite coatings prepared under normal temperature and atmospheric pressure are lower than those of (111) and (200) faces of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings prepared under supercritical CO2. However, the diffraction intensity of Ni-GQDs-Ⅳ nanocomposite coating (220) face prepared at normal temperature and pressure is higher than that of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coating (220) face prepared under supercritical conditions, and the diffraction intensity of Ni-GQDs-Ⅳ nanocomposite coating (220) face shows a preferential orientation. Due to the low conductivity and solubility of metal salts in supercritical CO2, when supercritical CO2 comes into contact with the cathode, it impedes crystal growth, thus providing time for atoms to migrate to the most stable position during electrodeposition, so the preferred orientation of nickel crystals is the (111) face. However, under normal temperature and pressure conditions, there is no supercritical CO2 to hinder the growth of crystals. Atoms cannot migrate to stable positions, so the preferred orientation of Ni crystals is (220) face.

The average grain size of nickel grains with different coatings was calculated using the diffraction peak width and Debye-Scherrer [33] formula, as shown below.
(1)
D =
K γ
B C O S θ

In the formula, Scherrer constant K is 0.89, X-ray wavelength γ is 0.154056 nm, B is the half-height and width of the diffraction peak, and θ is the diffraction Angle. The grain size of the coating is obtained from formula (1), and the results are shown in Table 6 .

It can be seen from Table 6 that under supercritical conditions, the grain size of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coating first decreases and then increases with the increase of reverse pulse current density, in which the grain size of Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ is the smallest (4.58 nm), smaller than that of Ni-GQDs-Ⅰ and Ni-GQDs-Ⅲ. The grain size is much smaller than that of Ni-GQDs-Ⅳ, and the grain size is reduced by 75.3 %. Since the reverse pulse current density of 0.8 A/dm2 has the best dissolution effect on the surface of the coating, the cathode ion concentration at this time is more suitable for the larger forward pulse current in the next cathode cycle to play a role, so the electrodeposited coating is the most flat and dense, the lowest porosity, the tightest intergranular bond, and therefore the grain size is small. At normal temperature and atmospheric pressure, the maximum grain size of Ni-GQDs-Ⅳ nanocomposite coating is 18.55 nm. The reason for the above phenomenon is that at normal temperature and atmospheric pressure, there is no supercritical CO2 to prevent grain growth. In addition, in the absence of supercritical CO2 dispersed graphene quantum dots (GQDs), the GQDs were not uniformly distributed in the coatings and did not form more nucleation sites to prevent Ni grain growth, resulting in larger grain sizes in the coatings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings prepared by different processes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 shows the Raman spectra of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Raman spectra of Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coatings prepared under supercritical CO2 and Ni-GQDs-Ⅳ nanocomposite coatings prepared under normal temperature and atmospheric pressure. Table 7 shows the positions of D and G bands, the intensity values, and the ratios of D and G peaks in the Raman spectra. It is known that incomplete graphite microcrystals cause the D-peak, many structural defects, and unsaturated edge carbon atoms, and the D-peak represents the defects of the crystal lattice, so the larger the value, the more defects of the C atom crystal, the G-peak strength can be used to characterize the integrity of the SP2 hybrid bond structure in the graphite structure. The intensity of the G-peak reflects the material's number of layers and the crystal lattice's integrity. The quality of GQDs is determined by the D-band and G-band intensity ratio ID/IG. The smaller the ratio of ID/IG, the better the quality of GQDs.

From Fig. 6 and Table 7, it can be seen that the ratio of the D peak intensity to the G peak intensity of the Ni-GQDs-II nanocomposite coating is 0.6538. The ratio of D-peak strength to G-peak strength of GQDs is 0.8437, and the ratio of D-peak strength to G-peak strength of GQDs is higher than that of Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coating. In Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coating, the defects of GQDs are reduced, and the quality of GQDs is improved, possibly because the integrity of GQDs is improved by the action of supercritical CO2. No D and G peaks were detected in Ni-GQDs-Ⅳ composite coating. It may be because GQDs are easy to agglomerate during electrodeposition at normal temperature and atmospheric pressure, so the binding effect of GQDs and Ni ions is poor during electrodeposition. GQDs are unevenly distributed and make up a relatively small proportion of the coating. Therefore, it is not easy to detect the presence of GQDs in Ni-GQDs-Ⅳ nanocomposite coatings by Raman spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reverse pulse current density on properties of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coating
Influence of reverse pulse current density on the microhardness of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coating
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 shows the microhardness of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings prepared by different processes. As can be seen from Fig. 7, under the condition of supercritical CO2, the Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coating prepared with a reverse current density of 0.8 A/dm2 has a higher hardness of 867.2 HV. Compared with Ni-GQDs-Ⅳ nanocomposite coating prepared at normal temperature and atmospheric pressure, the hardness is significantly increased by 53.7 %. Because when the reverse pulse current density is 0.8 A/dm2, the cathode dissolution effect is most suitable. At this time, the ion concentration near the cathode is higher, which is conducive to the higher forward pulse current density in the subsequent cathode cycle. Higher forward pulse current density can make the nucleation rate larger than the crystal growth rate and the grain size smaller. At the same time, supercritical CO2 can effectively refine the grain [34], so the grain size of Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coating is smaller. According to the Hall-Petch relationship [35], the presence of small grains obstructs the movement of the dislocation and effectively improves the hardness of the coating. The surface of the coating is compact and flat, and the porosity is low, so the Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coating has a higher hardness.

Compared with coatings prepared by other processes. The microhardness of Ni-GQDs-IV nanocomposite coatings prepared at normal temperature and atmospheric pressure was even lower at 564.4 HV. Because at normal temperature and atmospheric pressure. There was no supercritical CO2-assisted electrodeposition, so the growth of the coating crystals failed to be limited. In addition, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are easy to agglomerate during electrodeposition, their distribution in the coating is not uniform, and they cannot form more nucleation points to prevent the growth of grains. Therefore, the Ni-GQDs-Ⅳ nanocomposite coating has a larger grain size. Therefore, the combination between grains is not tight enough, and there are more voids between grains. Moreover, the microstructure density of the coating is poor, the surface is uneven, and the porosity is high, so the hardness of the coating is low.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reverse pulse current density on surface roughness of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coating
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 shows the surface roughness of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings prepared by different processes. As can be seen from Fig. 8, the roughness of Ni-GQDs-II nanocomposite coatings prepared under supercritical CO2 condition with reverse pulse current density of 0.8A /dm2 is lower, which is 0.236 μm. Compared with that of the Ni-GQDs-IV nanocomposite coatings prepared at normal temperature and atmospheric pressure, the roughness of Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coatings is significantly reduced by 37 %. Since the reverse pulse current density of 0.8 A /dm2 has the best dissolution effect on bumps, burrs, nodules, and other defects on the coating surface, the cathode ion concentration is higher at this time, which is conducive to playing a higher role in the next cathode cycle. In the electrodeposition process, the formation rate of the crystal nucleus is greater than the growth rate of the crystal, and the growth of the grain is hindered. When supercritical CO2 comes into contact with the cathode, grain growth is also hindered. At this time, the degree of grain refinement is higher. Therefore, the grain size of the coating is small. The surface of the coating is flat and dense, so the roughness of the coating is low.

Under normal temperature and atmospheric pressure conditions, there is no supercritical CO2 assist during electrodeposition, and the grains cannot be refined effectively. Moreover, the second phase additive graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are easy to agglomerate, which is unevenly distributed in the coating, and there are no more nucleation sites to limit the growth of Ni grains. Currently, the grains are coarse, and there are more intergranular Spaces. The results show that the Ni-GQDs-Ⅳ nanocomposite coating has poor microstructure density, uneven surface, and high porosity. Therefore, the surface roughness of the coating is high.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reverse pulse current density on wear resistance of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coating
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 shows the friction coefficient of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings prepared by different processes. As can be seen from Fig. 9, the friction coefficient of Ni-GQDs-Ⅰ, Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ, Ni-GQDs-Ⅲ, and Ni-GQDs-Ⅳ nanocomposite coatings changes with the change of friction and wear time. The friction curve of the coating includes the process of removing the roughness of the coating surface and flattening the uneven coating. This stage is the initial run-in stage. At this time, the contact area between the surface of the coating and the steel ball is small, and the stress concentration and plastic deformation occur in some uneven positions. When the surface of the coating becomes smooth, the wear enters the stable stage and shows a stable friction coefficient, and the final friction coefficient is stable at 0.314, 0.262, 0.299, and 0.361. It is well known that the smaller the coefficient of friction, the better the wear resistance [36]. Among the coatings prepared with different process parameters, the friction coefficient of Ni-GQDs-II nanocomposite coating is smaller at 0.262, which is significantly reduced by 27.4 %. Therefore, the wear resistance of Ni-GQDs-II nanocomposite coating is better. The friction coefficients of Ni-GQDs-Ⅰ nanocomposite coatings and Ni-GQDs-Ⅲ nanocomposite coatings are similar, but they have higher friction coefficients, so the wear resistance is poor. The Ni-GQDs-IV nanocomposite coating has a higher coefficient of friction compared to other coatings, so the wear resistance is worse.

Archard's law [37] is shown in formula (2), where Q is the wear rate, K is the friction coefficient, W is the applied load (N), and H is the hardness. According to Archard's law, under the same wear conditions, the higher the hardness, the better the wear resistance. By comparing the hardness of the four coatings, it is found that the hardness of Ni-GQDs-II nanocomposite coating is higher. The hardness of Ni-GQDs-I nanocomposite coating is not much different from that of Ni-GQDs-III nanocomposite coating, but both are lower than that of Ni-GQDs-II nanocomposite coating. The hardness of Ni-GQDs-IV nanocomposite coating is much lower than the other coatings. Since the hardness of Ni-GQDs-II nanocomposite coating is higher, the wear resistance of the coating is better.
(2)
Q =
KW
H

After friction and wear experiments, the cross-section and three-dimensional surface of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings prepared by different processes are shown in Fig. 10 . The maximum depth, cross-sectional area, and volume wear of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings prepared by different processes are shown in Table 8 . According to Fig. 10 and Table 8, compared with other coatings, the wear traces of Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coatings are relatively shallow. The cross-sectional area and volume of wear marks are relatively small. The volumetric wear of Ni-GQDs-II nanocomposite coating is 3.395 × 107 µm3, which is 74.7 %, 65.5 %, and 42.1 % of the volumetric wear of Ni-GQDs-I nanocomposite coating, Ni-GQDs-III nanocomposite coating, and Ni-GQDs-IV nanocomposite coating, respectively. The Ni-GQDs-II nanocomposite coating showed less volumetric wear compared to other coatings, with up to 57.9 % reduction. Due to the small volume wear of Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coatings, they have excellent wear resistance.

The reason for the above phenomenon is that the cathodic dissolution effect of the reverse pulse current is poor when the reverse pulse current density is 0.3 A/dm2 under supercritical CO2 conditions, resulting in nodular and raised defects on the surface of Ni-GQDs-Ⅰ nanocomposite coatings. This leads to poor surface flatness, high roughness, and low hardness of the coating. As a result, the resistance of the coating to the shear force during friction contact is low, so the friction coefficient of the coating is high, and the volume wear is large. When the reverse pulse current density is 1.3 A/dm2, the cathode dissolution effect of the reverse pulse current density is stronger. The cathode ion concentration is higher at this time, resulting in massive spalling and pitting defects on the surface of Ni-GQDs-Ⅲ nanocomposite coating, resulting in poor surface flatness, high porosity, high roughness, and low hardness of the coating. As a result, the resistance of the coating to the shear force during friction contact is low, so the friction coefficient of the coating is high, and the volume wear is large. When the reverse pulse current density is 0.8 A/dm2, the cathodic dissolution effect of the reverse pulse current is good, and the surface of Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coating is flat and dense. Moreover, the roughness of the coating is small and the hardness is high. As a result, the coating has a strong resistance to the shear force generated by friction and wear. As a result, the coefficient of friction of the coating is relatively low. The volume wear of the coating is minimal. At normal temperature and atmospheric pressure conditions, because there is no supercritical CO2-assisted electrodeposition, the grain size of the Ni-GQDs-IV nanocomposite coating is relatively large, and the bonding effect between the grains is poor. This leads to defects such as protrusions, pores, and cracks on the surface of the coating and poor surface quality. Moreover, due to the high roughness and low hardness of the coating. As a result, the coating has low resistance to shear forces generated by friction and wear. As a result, the coating has a high coefficient of friction and volumetric wear.

Fig. 11 shows the 1500-fold SEM image of the wear marks of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings prepared by different processes. In the friction and wear experiment, the steel ball with higher hardness will produce plowing on the surface of the coating. It can be seen from Fig. 11 (a) that the surface quality of Ni-GQDs-Ⅰ nanocomposite coating wear is poor, and there are many defects on the surface of the wear, including obvious large-area adhesive wear and some faults. Due to the low reverse pulse current density during the preparation of Ni-GQDs-Ⅰ nanocomposite coating, the coating is uneven and dense, with high porosity and low hardness. The lower hardness leads to poor support load effect of the coating in the friction and wear test, which enhances the plowing effect of the steel ball on the coating, so the surface topography of the wear marks is poor. From Fig. 11 (b), it can be seen that the wear degree of Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coating is relatively low, and there are no obvious defects on the surface of the wear marks, only some plastic flow. The reverse pulse current density used at this time is suitable for preparing coatings with dense and flat surfaces, low porosity, and high hardness. High hardness has a good supporting effect on the load in friction and reduces the plowing effect of the steel ball on the coating in friction and wear tests, so the surface morphology of the coating wear marks is good.

From Fig. 11 (c), it can be seen that the surface morphology of the Ni-GQDs-III nanocomposite coating wear marks is poor, with some cracks and wear adhesion defects on the surface of the wear marks. Due to the excessive reverse pulse current density during electrodeposition, there is much blocky peeling and surface pitting on the surface of the coating, resulting in a higher porosity of the coating. The surface of the coating is uneven, with poor surface tissue density and low hardness. The lower hardness results in poor support of the coating for load in friction and wear experiments, enhancing the plowing effect of the steel ball on the coating and resulting in poor surface morphology of wear marks. From Fig. 11 (d), it can be seen that the surface quality of the Ni-GQDs-IV nanocomposite coating abrasion marks is poor, with a large number of blemishes and wear attachments on the surface. Compared with other coatings, the surface morphology of the abrasion marks is poor. This is because there is no supercritical CO2-assisted electrodeposition at normal temperature and atmospheric pressure, resulting in the failure of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to be effectively dispersed. Therefore, the distribution of GQDs in the coating is uneven and the content is low. Because the aggregation of GQDs in the coating increases the adhesion area of the friction surface, the adhesive wear of the coating becomes stronger as the adhesion effect increases. The surface of the coating is uneven, with low tissue density as well as high porosity. Also, because of the low hardness of the coating, the supporting load in the friction and wear experiments is poor. At this time, the plowing effect of the steel ball on the coating is stronger, so the surface morphology of the coating abrasion marks is poorer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reverse pulse current density on corrosion resistance of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coating
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 12 shows the electrochemical corrosion results of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings prepared by different processes in a 3.5 % NaCl solution. Fig. 12 (a) shows the Tafel polarization curve of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coating. The self-corrosion voltage and self-corrosion current density of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings prepared by different processes can be obtained from the Tafel polarization curves, and the data are shown in Table 9 .

From Fig. 12(a) and Table 9, it can be seen that the self-corrosion voltage of the Ni-GQDs-II nanocomposite coating is higher at −139 mV. The self-corrosion voltage is significantly increased by 68.7 % compared to that of the Ni-GQDs-IV nanocomposite coating. The self-corrosion voltage reflects the stability of the coating surface, and the higher the self-corrosion voltage, the better the stability of the coating surface and the stronger the corrosion resistance of the coating surface. Therefore, the Ni-GQDs-II nanocomposite coating has superior corrosion resistance. Since the self-corrosion current is caused by the dissolution of the material, the lower the self-corrosion current density and the higher the charge transfer resistance, the better the corrosion resistance of the coating. From Fig. 12(a) and Table 9, it can be seen that among the four coatings, the self-corrosion current density of the Ni-GQDs-II nanocomposite coating is lower at 3.19 × 10−7 A/cm2, which is significantly reduced by 71.2 % compared with that of the Ni-GQDs-IV nanocomposite coating. It is known that the higher the self-corrosion voltage and the lower the self-corrosion current density of a coating, the better the corrosion resistance of the coating [38]. Because Ni-GQDs-II nanocomposite coating has higher self-corrosion voltage and lower self-corrosion current density, the corrosion resistance is more excellent.

In order to further compare and analyze the corrosion resistance of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings, EIS experiments were conducted on Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings, and the experimental results were fitted. Fig. 12 (b) shows the Nyquist plots of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings, where solid symbols represent the experimental results and solid lines represent the fitting results. As shown in Fig. 12(b), among the four coatings, the impedance spectral radius of Ni-GQDs-II nanocomposite coating is larger, and the impedance spectral radius of Ni-GQDs-I nanocomposite coating and Ni-GQDs-III nanocomposite coating are not very much different from each other, but both of them are lower than that of Ni-GQDs-II nanocomposite coating. The impedance spectral radius of Ni-GQDs-IV nanocomposite coating is lower than that of other coatings. Because the larger the impedance spectral radius, the larger the impedance. The larger the impedance, the better the inhibition of electron and ion transport, thus inhibiting the occurrence of electrochemical reactions, slowing down the rate of electrochemical reactions on the surface of the coating, and reducing the degree of oxidation and corrosion on the surface of the coating. It is known that the higher the impedance, the better the corrosion resistance [39]. Since the Ni-GQDs-II nanocomposite coating has relatively high impedance, it has more excellent corrosion resistance.

Subsequently, the EIS experimental results were fitted and analyzed using the equivalent circuit model shown in Fig. 12 (c). The fitting parameters are shown in Table 9. From Table 9, it can be seen that the R ct value of Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coating is relatively large, at 31594.53 Ω·cm2. The dispersion index N dl value is relatively high, at 0.862. Compared with the Ni-GQDs-IV nanocomposite coating, the R ct value and N dl value of the Ni-GQDs-II nanocomposite coating were significantly increased by 226.2 % and 67.1 %, respectively. The higher the R ct value, the more difficult it is for the electrochemical anodic dissolution reaction to occur. The N dl value represents the density of the oxide film on the coating, and the higher the N dl value, the greater the density of the oxide film. The higher the R ct and N dl values, the better the corrosion resistance of the coating. The R ct and N dl values of Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coatings are higher, so they have excellent corrosion resistance.

Fig. 13 shows the surface 1000-fold SEM image of Ni-GQDs nanocomposite coatings prepared by different processes after immersion and corrosion in 3.5 % NaCl solution for 120 h. From Fig. 13 (a), it can be seen that the Ni-GQDs-Ⅰ nanocomposite coating shows large corrosion pits on the coating after being corroded by corrosive media such as Cl−, with a larger diameter and some corrosion points, indicating poor corrosion resistance of the coating. The Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coating shown in Fig. 13 (b) has only some corrosion points on the surface after corrosion, and the surface morphology after corrosion is good, indicating that the coating has good corrosion resistance. The Ni-GQDs-Ⅲ nanocomposite coating shown in Fig. 13 (c) showed more corrosion pits on the surface after corrosion, and the diameter of the corrosion pits was larger than that of the Ni-GQDs-Ⅰ nanocomposite coating, indicating that the corrosion resistance of the Ni GQDs-Ⅲ nanocomposite coating was worse. The Ni-GQDs-IV nanocomposite coating shown in Fig. 13 (d) showed large corrosion pits on the surface after corrosion. The diameter of the corrosion pits was smaller than that of the Ni-GQDs-III nanocomposite coating, but the depth was deeper, indicating that the corrosion resistance of the Ni-GQDs-IV nanocomposite coating was poor. The above results show that the Ni-GQDs-II nanocomposite coating has better corrosion resistance.

The reasons for the above phenomenon are as follows: when the reverse pulse current density is 0.3 A/dm2, the current density is small, and the optimization effect on the coating during the electrodeposition process is small, resulting in poor microstructure density and high porosity of the coating. In this way, the corrosive medium is easier to penetrate into the coating, so the corrosion resistance of Ni-GQDs-Ⅰ nanocomposite coating is poor. When the reverse pulse current density was increased to 0.8 A/dm2, the coating quality was optimized to a greater extent due to the reverse pulse current. The coating has a higher tissue density and lower porosity. Moreover, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are uniformly dispersed on the surface of the coating, which can effectively reduce the anodic dissolution area and serve as a physical barrier to prevent corrosion of the coating by corrosive media. Therefore, the corrosion resistance of Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coating is good. When the reverse pulse current density was increased to 1.3 A/dm2, the higher reverse pulse current density produced a strong cathodic dissolution effect on the surface of the coating, resulting in a high concentration of ions near the cathode and a poor electrodeposition effect. Defects such as lumpy spalling and pitting appear on the coating surface. Also because of the low tissue density and high porosity of the coating. So the coating is less resistant to corrosive media. So the corrosion resistance of Ni-GQDs-III nanocomposite coating is poor. When electrodeposited at normal temperature and atmospheric pressure, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) tend to agglomerate due to the lack of assistance from supercritical CO2. This leads to uneven distribution of GQDs in the coating and poor shielding against corrosive media. In addition, due to the relatively large grains in the coating. This leads to a low density of the organizational structure of the coating and a high porosity. At this time, the corrosive medium is relatively easy to penetrate into the coating, so the corrosion resistance of the Ni-GQDs-IV nanocomposite coating is poor.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coating prepared with reverse pulse current density of 0.8A /dm2 under supercritical CO2 conditions has a compact and flat surface, no obvious defects on the surface, and excellent surface quality. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and Ni ions are uniformly distributed in Ni-GQDs-Ⅱ nanocomposite coating.
Supercritical CO2 effectively dispersed the graphene quantum dots and greatly restricted the grain growth. The grain size was smaller at the appropriate reverse pulse current density.

The variation of process parameters has a large impact on the mechanical properties of the deposited layer. The mechanical properties of the deposited layer were improved by 53.7 % under more appropriate process parameters.

The corrosion resistance of the deposited layers was also affected by the variation of process parameters. When graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were uniformly dispersed in the coating, the grain refinement of the deposited layer was higher. The corrosion resistance of the deposited layer increased by 61.2 %.
